Question: Before May 20, 2024, will it be announced that Minouche Shafik will no longer be President of Columbia University?
llm_reasoning: As of May 1, 2024, Shafik was under severe and multidirectional pressure after the encampment, arrests, and Hamilton Hall occupation, so an abrupt exit was clearly plausible, not remote. But the key determinant for a public departure announcement before May 20 was whether Columbia’s trustees had turned against her, and the strongest visible signal still pointed the other way: the board had publicly backed her on April 24, she had chosen escalation rather than retreat, and her move to keep NYPD on campus through May 17 suggested the institution expected to finish the semester and commencement under her leadership. Question: Will the US Congress pass a bill implementing Trump's tariff policies in 2025?
llm_reasoning: As of February 1, 2025, a congressional bill implementing Trump’s tariff policies looks clearly possible but still distinctly less likely than not, mainly because Trump is already moving on tariffs through executive authority, which lets Republicans get the policy without forcing vulnerable members to cast a direct pro-tariff vote; that sharply reduces the practical need for legislation. The main plausible YES path is a reconciliation bill that uses legislated tariff revenue to help offset extension of the 2017 tax cuts, and that path is real because Republicans control the House and Senate and tariff options were reportedly on the Ways and Means menu. But the obstacles are substantial: House margins are razor-thin, Senate Republicans from agriculture and import-sensitive states are already voicing skepticism about broad tariffs, fiscal hawks are blocking agreement even on the larger tax package, Democrats are unlikely to help, and adding tariff language would make an already difficult bill harder. Put together, that leaves a meaningful but still modest chance of passage in 2025.

Question: Will Ali Khamenei cease to be supreme leader of Iran in 2024?
llm_reasoning: Khamenei’s age made a 2024 exit materially more likely than for most incumbents, but the overwhelming driver was still death or a clearly incapacitating health event, not politics. As of January 21, 2024, he had ruled since 1989, continued to appear publicly, and sat atop a system designed for regime continuity, elite coordination, and suppression of unrest, which made resignation very unlikely and forced removal from within the regime a low-probability path absent a major shock. Domestic discontent, succession chatter, and regional escalation added some tail risk, but not enough to outweigh the basic fact that authoritarian leaders in entrenched systems usually remain until death or unmistakable incapacity. That leaves a meaningful but still fairly modest chance over the remaining year, around one in eight.

Question: Will Benjamin Netanyahu remain Prime Minister of Israel throughout 2024?
llm_reasoning: Netanyahu looks more likely than not to remain prime minister for all of 2024 because the mechanisms that could remove him are real but still comparatively hard to trigger as of January 21, 2024: Israel is in an active war, which usually dampens near-term leadership change; no regular election is due in 2024; his core right-wing and ultra-Orthodox partners have strong incentives to avoid an election they could lose; and Likud has a long record of sticking with him through crises. Against that, he is politically damaged by the October 7 failure, public trust is weak, protests and accountability demands are likely to return with force, and a coalition rupture, postwar commission shock, or internal party move could end his tenure before year-end. Balancing those factors, I would put the chance that he stays in office continuously through December 31, 2024 at about two-thirds rather than near-certainty.

Balancing the unusually high wartime hazard against the still-high bar for a serious radiological accident over an 11-month horizon, 18 is my estimate.

llm_reasoning: The probability is high because as of October 6, 2025 the Fed only needed one more 25 bp cut from the current 4.00%-4.25% range for this to resolve YES, and the weight of contemporaneous evidence pointed that way: the September FOMC statement explicitly emphasized rising downside labor-market risks, the September SEP median year-end funds rate of 3.6 implied further easing after the September cut, futures pricing had moved to an overwhelming expectation of an October cut by early October, and major bank forecasts were largely aligned on another move. Question: On May 18th, will Kyle, Texas set the world record for largest gathering of individuals with the same first name?
llm_reasoning: As of May 3, 2024, YES looks plausible but distinctly less likely than NO because Kyle, Texas must clear 2,325 eligible Kyles versus its own best turnout of 1,490 in 2023, so it needs about 836 more verified participants, a roughly 56% jump in a single year. The event is free, heavily promoted, and has real viral appeal, so a late surge is possible, but attendance still requires travel coordination, exact first-name matching, and ID/documentation that will exclude some would-be attendees. Since last year already drew broad attention and still fell well short of the Guinness mark, and there is no obvious pre-May-3 public evidence of registrations or momentum on the scale needed to make that leap more likely than not, the chance of success looks a bit under one in three.
